articles.title: Music is in the math that holds the universe together
articles.description: “How on Earth does a two-year-old naturally understand a musical beat?” (Issue #425) In Black Culture is Real! , writer LGWare, The Black Lens describes a scene about halfway through the 2025 movie Sinners where Sammie, a young blues musician played by Miles Caton, is called on stage at the Mississippi juke joint at the center of the film. Up until this point in the film, the audience has heard about the power and magic of music. And as Sammie begins to play his song , we see what that means: West African drummers emerge among the patrons; a 1970s rocker plays his electric guitar in sight of a 1930s banjo player and a 1980s DJ; B-boys and ballerinas move with West African and Chinese xiqu dancers. “His performance rips open time and space, allowing dancers and performers from across time and history to join the party,” LGWare writes. “It was a Black culture across time.” Music isn’t just a connection to our past and future. As several Medium writers have recently explored, music is deeply ingrained in our language, our technology, and even with the forces that power the universe. The more intention we have with our music taste the better off we will be. — Stanley C. , How Should We Address Cultural Appropriation In The New Global Music Industry? The cosmic symphony “How on Earth does a two-year-old naturally understand a musical beat?” asks engineer and teacher Marco Capriz . It’s not just something that’s innately human or an outgrowth of civilization, he argues. The internal rhythm that helps a toddler innately clap along to a song is governed by the same mathematical rules that hold the universe together. In The Cosmic Symphony: Why the Laws of Physics Sound Like Music , Capriz explores the connection between physics and music. Ancient mathematician Pythagoras (of Pythagorean Theorem fame) first named the concept of Musica Universalis — that if musical intervals that can be described with math, and math is what governs the movements of planetary bodies, then the universe must also must produce music. Astronomer Johannes Kepler took this concept one step further, writing music for a “Solar System Choir” in which each planet is assigned a scale. For centuries, this was considered “as a beautiful poetic metaphor,” writes Capriz. “We viewed it as a relic of a time before ‘hard’ science.” But it turns out that there is a kernel of truth to Pythagoras’ insight: “We are discovering that rhythm is not just an art form we created for entertainment; it is a byproduct of stable physical systems.” It’s a concept physicists are calling Orbital Resonance : “When two or more orbiting bodies exert a regular, periodic gravitational influence on each other, they eventually settle into a ‘resonant chain’ — a series of small-integer ratios like 1:2 or 2:3. In a very literal sense, the solar system is a vast collection of coupled oscillators. The same “objective physical coupling” that explains why we tap our feet is what prevents the moons of Jupiter from crashing into each other or drifting off into the void.” Modern musicians and physicists alike have started translating the resonance of the universe into music through a process called sonification — you can listen to some of that music here . The result was “100 Seconds of Pi,” one of my earliest sonifications. I think of it as a collaboration of sorts. Specifically, my intent was to allow π to provide the core material and see just how little I could do to produce music that might pass for being entirely composed and performed by humans. — Harlan Brothers , Pi: A Musical Celebration The language of popular music A few issues back, the Medium Newsletter took a look at the impact of language and how certain words can shape our thinking. In a recent piece for Language Lab , William Sidnam argues that language also shapes the type of music a culture produces. Native French speakers, for example, are renowned today for their contributions to electronic music with acts like Justice or Daft Punk . Meanwhile, English speakers are known for their rock & roll. Sidnam argues that this difference has a lot to do with the actual construction of the language. “One issue with French is that it’s syllable-timed, meaning that in French, every syllable is roughly equivalent in length,” he writes. Meanwhile, in English the syllable lengths vary. Sidnam argues that these differences make them ideal fits for specific music genres. French is naturally disposed to the smooth beats of electronica, while English electronic music (like Aphex Twin ) tends to sound more “rhythmically chaotic.” English’s variable syllable length makes it a perfect fit for rock’s emphasis on the second and fourth beat. Sidnam notes this pattern across cultures: West African speech patterns are thought to have influenced jazz; German classical music tends to emphasize structure and melody; people who speak tonal languages like Mandarin and Vietnamese are more likely to have perfect pitch. Each language brings its own strengths to the music it produces: “This all makes me wonder: Could a musician who grows up speaking multiple languages from multiple language families hypothetically compose music that’s more complex than anything that’s ever been written?” “The beauty of [South Korean boy band BTS] is its cultural roots, the way in which many members hold firmly and unapologetically onto it, and most importantly, to me, they stand at the turning point into another era of globalisation.” - Hannah's Café | Hug in a Mug , The Myth of the Native Speaker Bringing the human element back into music For some writers, the rise of new technology threatens to separate humans from the natural rhythms around us. As musician Jeffrey Anthony writes , people now discover songs through algorithms suited to their taste; A.I. tools are being used to generate music; an internet that favors video is incentivizing musicians to be technically proficient and visually interesting. Technology is creating new standards of polish beyond a human capability. But as session guitarist Elliot Randall (most famous for his guitar work on Reeling in the Years and Fame ) told Anthony early in his career , “You execute impeccably. But you don’t have anything to say.” One year ago, Anthony declared that the future of music was offline . To save what he called “the living middle,” he put his words into practice. He started to build an entirely offline music label, one that rejected what he calls the “ griddification ” of music and focused entirely on real-life releases. But as Anthony pitched his ideas to musicians around the country, he discovered that the relationship that artists have with the internet is much more complicated. As one artist said: “Listen, Jeffrey: we go back to the cities where we opened for Pearl Jam and play a sold-out club show for 150 to 200 fans, and every single one comes up after the show and asks, ‘Hey, I noticed you only released three songs from your record on Spotify. Where can I listen to the rest?’ And we say, ‘You can get the entire album for ten dollars on Bandcamp.’ And maybe two people purchase it. The rest simply say, ‘But when will the other songs be on Spotify?’” For Anthony, it was a revelation. “Offline distribution and embodied production proved to be two distinct problems,” he wrote. “You could record a song with full human timing, real-time musical coordination, and no grid correction, and still want people to hear it wherever they listen. Artists did not need another wall between them and their audience.” Instead, Anthony changed his focus to the second part of the problem and developed the Certificate of Embodied Production . In practical terms, it’s a certification that anyone can apply for, and it attests that a piece of music has “no grid correction, no pitch correction, and no generative AI... forms of algorithmic mediation that overwrite the temporal specificity of a human performance.” Or, as Anthony writes, “Think Certified Organic , but for the way sound recordings are made.” Rather than fully embracing the machine or going entirely offline, the real solution could be somewhere in the middle. [A]udience preference is shaped by repetition. If women are not played, they do not become familiar. If they are not familiar, they are considered commercially risky. The cycle reinforces itself. Data interrupts that cycle. — Linda Coogan Byrne , Counting the Silence: The Data That Proves Gender Bias in Music Interested in more writing about music? Check out publications The Riff , Rock N Heavy , or Music for Thought and see the top stories on the Music tag . Deepen your understanding every week with the Medium Newsletter. Sign up here . Edited and produced by brandon echter , Carly Rose Gillis , and Scott Lamb . Like what you see in this newsletter but not already a Medium member? Read without limits or ads, fund great writers, and join a community that believes in human storytelling . Music is in the math that holds the universe together was originally published in The Medium Blog on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

articles.title: Pathetic media outraged by Trump's attending White House Correspondents' Dinner
articles.description: Since 1921, the annual black-tie dinner has gathered members of the media, top politicos, and even Hollywood stars. In 1924 Calvin Coolidge launched what would become the tradition of the president speaking at the event. In a nod to the awkward social skills so prevalent among journalists, the event is known in DC as "the nerd prom."
